explanation: TRUE POSITIVE. The vulnerability is real and exploitable. In `_payFeeWhenTransfer` (lines 692-721), the per-address cap check at line 702 only validates `amountToTransfer` (net amount to `to`), but when `to == taxAddress`, the fee transfer at line 714 (`super._transfer(from, taxStorage.taxAddress, taxAmount)`) also deposits tokens into `taxAddress` without any cap check. The attacker calls `transfer(taxAddress, X)` where X is chosen so that `amountToTransfer ≤ cap` passes the check, but the fee `taxAmount` is silently deposited first (no check), and then `amountToTransfer` is delivered by the caller's `super.transfer()` — causing `taxAddress` to accumulate `taxAmount + amountToTransfer = X > cap`. Concrete example: M=100 tokens cap, taxBPS=5000 (50%). Attacker calls `transfer(taxAddress, 200)`: cap check sees 100 ≤ 100 (passes), fee transfer deposits 100 tokens to taxAddress (no check), main transfer deposits another 100, final balance = 200 > 100 = cap. No upstream protection prevents `to == taxAddress`, no cap enforcement exists in `_update`, and `_taxAmount` only skips tax when the *sender* is taxAddress (not the recipient), so the tax still flows to taxAddress when it is the recipient. The fix requires adding `_checkMaxAmountPerAddress(taxStorage.taxAddress, taxAmount)` before the fee transfer, or combining both amounts when `to == taxAddress`.
